edges.description: Mutant TBP represses SP1-dependent transcription of cerebellar INPP5A, linking broad transcriptional dysregulation to selective Purkinje cell vulnerability.
edges.target: Cerebellar INPP5A loss drives Purkinje cell vulnerability

edges.description: Mutant TBP binds the CCAAT-box transcription factor NFY more tightly, sequestering NFYA and reducing NFY occupancy of chaperone gene promoters, leading to lower Hsp70/Hsp25/HspA5 expression and a blunted heat shock response.

Mutant TBP shows decreased association with the unfolded protein response transcription factor XBP1s, reducing transcription of the Purkinje-enriched neurotrophic factor MANF and removing a cell-type-specific neuroprotective signal.

Expanded polyglutamine TBP becomes a substrate for calpain proteolysis, generating C-terminal fragments that mislocalize and seed aggregation. Transcriptome analysis suggests synaptogenesis and calcium-signaling perturbations as a potential cause of elevated calpain activity, but the bridging intermediates are not definitively established.
Astrocytic mutant TBP activates inflammatory signaling and is required, together with neuronal mutant TBP, for severe neurodegeneration.
edges.target: Astrocyte-mediated NF-kappaB neuroinflammation amplifies neuronal injury

edges.description: Mutant TBP also causes degeneration of the caudate-putamen circuit in parallel with cerebellar pathology. Reduced postsynaptic D2 receptor binding and striatal hypometabolism in presymptomatic mutation carriers indicate that striatal vulnerability is an intrinsic, early consequence of the same polyQ-TBP transcriptional dysfunction rather than a downstream effect of cerebellar disease.

Loss of cerebellar INPP5A elevates IP3 levels and disrupts calcium signaling, and is sufficient on its own to cause Purkinje cell degeneration. Restoring INPP5A in SCA17 knock-in mice ameliorates Purkinje cell loss.
edges.source: Cerebellar INPP5A loss drives Purkinje cell vulnerability

edges.description: Glial NF-kappaB and ERK activation drive cytokine production and apoptotic signaling that promote Purkinje cell death; blocking astrocytic NF-kappaB ameliorates neurodegeneration in SCA17 models.
edges.source: Astrocyte-mediated NF-kappaB neuroinflammation amplifies neuronal injury

edges.description: Calpain-generated cytoplasmic TBP fragments are aggregation-prone and accumulate when protein-clearance pathways cannot remove them efficiently.
edges.target: Impaired proteostasis promotes mutant TBP aggregation

edges.description: Accumulating mutant TBP species drive Purkinje cell vacuolation and death. Enhancing chaperone and autophagy pathways reduces insoluble mutant TBP, vacuolation, and Purkinje cell loss in SCA17 mice.
intermediate_mechanisms: mutant TBP aggregation drives cellular dysfunction (transcriptional sequestration, ER stress)
cellular dysfunction triggers intrinsic apoptotic signaling in Purkinje neurons
edges.source: Impaired proteostasis promotes mutant TBP aggregation

edges.description: Reduced chaperone capacity (lower Hsp70/Hsp25/HspA5 and a blunted heat shock response) limits the cell's ability to refold or clear misfolded mutant TBP, allowing aggregation-prone species to accumulate.
edges.target: Impaired proteostasis promotes mutant TBP aggregation

edges.description: MANF overexpression rescues mutant TBP-mediated Purkinje cell degeneration via PKC-dependent signaling, demonstrating that the MANF deficit is a proximate, modifiable cause of selective Purkinje cell death in SCA17.
Convergent polyQ-driven toxicity in cerebellar-enriched pathways causes progressive Purkinje neuron loss, the cellular substrate of cerebellar ataxia in SCA17.

edges.target: Impaired proteostasis promotes mutant TBP aggregation

nodes.description: Astrocytic mutant TBP contributes non-cell-autonomously to neuronal loss. Severe degeneration requires mutant TBP in both neurons and glia, and inflammatory signaling in astrocytes, particularly NF-kappaB pathway activation, augments neurotoxicity. In SCA17 transgenic cerebellum, activation of MAPK/ERK in astrocytes and Bergmann glia precedes Purkinje cell loss, indicating that gliosis-driven signaling is an upstream contributor to neuronal apoptosis rather than a late reactive response.
nodes.id: Astrocyte-mediated NF-kappaB neuroinflammation amplifies neuronal injury

evidence.reference_title: Synergistic Toxicity of Polyglutamine-Expanded TATA-Binding Protein in Glia and Neuronal Cells: Therapeutic Implications for Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: We found that mutant TBP expression in neuronal cells or astrocytes alone only caused mild neurodegeneration, whereas severe neuronal toxicity requires the expression of mutant TBP in both neuronal and glial cells.

evidence.reference_title: Synergistic Toxicity of Polyglutamine-Expanded TATA-Binding Protein in Glia and Neuronal Cells: Therapeutic Implications for Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: We identified activated inflammatory signaling pathways in mutant TBP-expressing astrocytes, and blocking nuclear factor κB (NF-κB) signaling in astrocytes ameliorated neurodegeneration.

nodes.description: Calcium-dependent calpain proteolysis cleaves expanded TBP, generating C-terminal fragments that mislocalize to the cytoplasm and create aggregation-prone TBP species.

evidence.reference_title: Calpains as novel players in the molecular pathogenesis of spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: Here, we demonstrate that calpains cleave TBP, and emerging C-terminal fragments mislocalize to the cytoplasm.

evidence.reference_title: Calpains as novel players in the molecular pathogenesis of spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: Pharmacological or genetic calpain inhibition reduced TBP cleavage and aggregation, consequently improving cell viability.

nodes.description: Cerebellar neurons are particularly vulnerable to mutant TBP. In the cerebellum, mutant TBP suppresses SP1-dependent INPP5A expression, perturbing IP3-linked calcium signaling in a region enriched for INPP5A and promoting Purkinje cell degeneration.
nodes.id: Cerebellar INPP5A loss drives Purkinje cell vulnerability

evidence.reference_title: Cerebellum-enriched protein INPP5A contributes to selective neuropathology in mouse model of spinocerebellar ataxias type 17.
evidence.snippet: By expressing mutant TBP in different brain regions in adult wild-type mice via stereotaxic injection of adeno-associated virus, we found that adult cerebellar neurons are particularly vulnerable to mutant TBP.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebellum-enriched protein INPP5A contributes to selective neuropathology in mouse model of spinocerebellar ataxias type 17.
evidence.snippet: In SCA17 knock-in mice, mutant TBP inhibits SP1-mediated gene transcription to down-regulate INPP5A, a protein that is highly abundant in the cerebellum.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebellum-enriched protein INPP5A contributes to selective neuropathology in mouse model of spinocerebellar ataxias type 17.
evidence.snippet: CRISPR/Cas9-mediated deletion of Inpp5a in the cerebellum of wild-type mice leads to Purkinje cell degeneration, and Inpp5a overexpression decreases inositol 1,4,5-trisphosphate (IP3) levels and ameliorates Purkinje cell degeneration in SCA17 knock-in mice.

nodes.description: Convergent target of the cerebellar arm of SCA17 pathophysiology. Selective loss of cerebellar Purkinje cells, with accompanying basket and stellate cell pathology and granule layer thinning, produces the cerebellar atrophy seen on imaging and the cerebellar motor syndrome of SCA17 (gait ataxia, appendicular ataxia, and cerebellar dysarthria). Multiple upstream mechanisms - INPP5A loss, MANF/chaperone deficit, glial inflammatory signaling, and impaired proteostasis - converge on intrinsic apoptotic death of Purkinje neurons. Restoring any one upstream node (Inpp5a, chaperone activity, MANF, or NF-kappaB blockade) is partially protective.

evidence.reference_title: A novel transgenic rat model for spinocerebellar ataxia type 17 recapitulates neuropathological changes and supplies in vivo imaging biomarkers.
evidence.snippet: Neuropathologically, the severe phenotype of SCA17 rats was associated with neuronal loss, particularly in the cerebellum.

evidence.reference_title: Neuroprotective effects of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor in a novel transgenic mouse model of SCA17.
evidence.snippet: Purkinje cell degeneration was identified by immunostaining of calbindin and IP3R1. Reactive gliosis and neuroinflammation occurred in the transgenic cerebellum, accompanied by up-regulation of GFAP and Iba1.

evidence.reference_title: PET and MRI reveal early evidence of neurodegeneration in spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: MRI volumetry revealed atrophy of the cerebellum and caudate nucleus in manifesting patients (P = 0.04 and 0.05, respectively) and in presymptomatic mutation carriers (P = 0.04 and 0.01, respectively).

nodes.description: Progressive cerebellar speech impairment is a typical feature of the disorder.

evidence.reference_title: From normal gait to loss of ambulation in 6 months: a novel presentation of SCA17.
evidence.snippet: SCA17 is clinically heterogeneous and typically presents with slowly evolving ataxia, dysarthria, dementia, depression, and other movement disorders such as chorea.

nodes.description: Preclinical disease-modifying candidate that improved neurologic function and reduced Purkinje cell pathology in transgenic SCA17 mice. This remains experimental and has not been established as human therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Neuroprotective effects of granulocyte-colony stimulating factor in a novel transgenic mouse model of SCA17.
evidence.snippet: Our results suggest that granulocyte-colony stimulating factor has a neuroprotective effect in these transgenic mice, ameliorating their neurological and behavioral deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Targeting the prodromal stage of spinocerebellar ataxia type 17 mice: G-CSF in the prevention of motor deficits via upregulating chaperone and autophagy levels.
evidence.snippet: Treatment with G-CSF at the pre-symptomatic stage improved the motor coordination of SCA17 mice and reduced the cell loss, insoluble mutant TBP protein, and vacuole formation in the Purkinje neurons of these mice.

nodes.description: Limited chaperone and autophagy capacity permits accumulation of aggregation-prone mutant TBP species, whereas boosting proteostasis pathways enhances aggregate clearance and improves survival.
nodes.id: Impaired proteostasis promotes mutant TBP aggregation

evidence.reference_title: Calpains as novel players in the molecular pathogenesis of spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: Pharmacological or genetic calpain inhibition reduced TBP cleavage and aggregation, consequently improving cell viability.

evidence.reference_title: Targeting the prodromal stage of spinocerebellar ataxia type 17 mice: G-CSF in the prevention of motor deficits via upregulating chaperone and autophagy levels.
evidence.snippet: Upregulation of chaperone and autophagy levels further enhances the clearance of mutant protein aggregation, slowing the progression of pathology in SCA17 mice.

nodes.description: Despite ubiquitous TBP expression, polyglutamine expansion preferentially causes cerebellar neurodegeneration because adult cerebellar neurons are more vulnerable to mutant TBP than striatal or cortical neurons. The selective vulnerability of Purkinje cells emerges from the convergent toxic effects of mutant TBP on tissue-enriched cerebellar proteins and pathways — including INPP5A-dependent calcium homeostasis, Purkinje-enriched MANF neurotrophic support, and glial NF-kB inflammatory amplification — that are disproportionately concentrated in the cerebellum.

evidence.reference_title: Cerebellum-enriched protein INPP5A contributes to selective neuropathology in mouse model of spinocerebellar ataxias type 17.
evidence.snippet: adult cerebellar neurons are particularly vulnerable to mutant TBP

nodes.description: Expanded CAG/CAA repeats in exon 3 of TBP lengthen the polyglutamine tract of a general transcription factor. Mutant TBP remains nuclear and perturbs gene expression through abnormal interactions with other transcriptional regulators, linking a ubiquitously expressed protein to selective neuronal dysfunction.

biological_processes: regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 17 (SCA17) is a rare autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disease caused by a CAG repeat expansion in the TATA-box binding protein gene (TBP).

evidence.reference_title: Transcriptional dysregulation of TrkA associates with neurodegeneration in spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: We found that the high-affinity nerve growth factor receptor, TrkA, is down-regulated by mutant TBP in cells.

evidence.reference_title: Transcriptional dysregulation of TrkA associates with neurodegeneration in spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: Mutant TBP binds more Sp1, reduces its occupancy of the TrkA promoter and inhibits the activity of the TrkA promoter.

nodes.description: Progressive gait and balance impairment is the core clinical feature and may be slowly progressive over years, although more rapid decline can occur in some patients with lower expanded alleles.

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: As expected, the majority of subjects were ataxic (21 out of 25, 84%).

evidence.reference_title: From normal gait to loss of ambulation in 6 months: a novel presentation of SCA17.
evidence.snippet: SCA17 is clinically heterogeneous and typically presents with slowly evolving ataxia, dysarthria, dementia, depression, and other movement disorders such as chorea.

nodes.description: Parallel to the cerebellar arm, mutant TBP also drives degeneration of the caudate-putamen circuit, which underlies the extrapyramidal and cognitive components of SCA17 (chorea, dystonia, parkinsonism, dementia). Striatal medium spiny neurons show postsynaptic dopaminergic dysfunction with reduced D2 receptor binding and decreased striatal glucose metabolism, and these abnormalities are detectable in presymptomatic mutation carriers, indicating that striatal vulnerability is an early and intrinsic feature rather than a late consequence of cerebellar disease.

evidence.reference_title: PET and MRI reveal early evidence of neurodegeneration in spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: (11)C-raclopride PET showed impairment of the postsynaptic dopaminergic compartment of the putamen and caudate nucleus not only in manifest SCA17 patients (P = 0.04 and 0.008, respectively) but also in yet-unaffected mutation carriers (P = 0.05 and 0.05, respectively).

evidence.reference_title: PET and MRI reveal early evidence of neurodegeneration in spinocerebellar ataxia type 17.
evidence.snippet: PET demonstrated decreased glucose metabolism in the striatum, as well as in the cuneus, cingulum, and parietal lobe, in all SCA17 patients and presymptomatic mutation carriers.

nodes.description: Multidisciplinary symptomatic management with physical therapy, speech therapy, mobility aids, swallow support, and psychiatric/cognitive care remains the standard of care.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular Mechanisms of Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 17.
evidence.snippet: At present, there is no effective treatment for SCA17, and existing therapies provide only symptomatic relief.

evidence.reference_title: Complexity of the Genetics and Clinical Presentation of Spinocerebellar Ataxia 17.
evidence.snippet: We found a negative linear correlation between total CAG repeat length and age at disease onset and, unlike SCA1, there was no correlation between the longest contiguous CAG tract and age at disease onset.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 17: report of a family with reduced penetrance of an unstable Gln49 TBP allele, haplotype analysis supporting a founder effect for unstable alleles and comparative analysis of SCA17 genotypes.
evidence.snippet: Observed pathogenic expansions ranged from 43-63 glutamine (Gln) codons (Gln43-63). Reduced penetrance is known for Gln43-48 alleles.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 17: report of a family with reduced penetrance of an unstable Gln49 TBP allele, haplotype analysis supporting a founder effect for unstable alleles and comparative analysis of SCA17 genotypes.
evidence.snippet: The 76-year-old father of two SCA17 patients carries the Gln49 TBP allele but presents without obvious neurological symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 17 (SCA17).
evidence.snippet: Second, compared to the other SCA subtypes caused by expanded trinucleotide repeats, anticipation in SCA17 kindreds is rare because of the characteristic structure of the TBP gene.

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-Phenotype Correlations for ATX-TBP (SCA17): MDSGene Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: Overall, 97.7% of the patients had ≥41 repeats, while 99.6% of patients with PD and 99.9% of healthy individuals had ≤42 repeats, with a gray zone of reduced penetrance between 41 and 45 repeats.

evidence.reference_title: Genotype-Phenotype Correlations for ATX-TBP (SCA17): MDSGene Systematic Review.
evidence.snippet: Pure parkinsonism was more common in ATX-TBP patients with 41 to 45 repeats than in the group with ≥46 repeats, which conversely more often presented with a complex phenotype with mixed movement disorders.

evidence.reference_title: The New Face of Dynamic Mutation - The CAA[CAG]n CAA CAG Motif as a Mutable Unit in the TBP Gene Causative for Spinocerebellar Ataxia Type 17.
evidence.snippet: Detailed analysis of the CAG/CAA repeat structure, not just the number of repeats, in TBP-expanded alleles should be performed, as it may have a prognostic value in the prediction of stability/instability during transmission and the possible anticipation of the disease.

nodes.description: Mutant TBP binds the CCAAT-binding transcription factor NFY more tightly, sequestering NFYA into mutant TBP aggregates and blocking NFY occupancy of chaperone gene promoters. The result is reduced Hsp70, Hsp25, and HspA5 expression and a blunted heat shock / unfolded protein response. With age, a constitutive decline in Hsc70 chaperone activity compounds this deficit and contributes to late-onset selective neuronal vulnerability.

biological_processes: regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: Neuronal expression of TATA box-binding protein containing expanded polyglutamine in knock-in mice reduces chaperone protein response by impairing the function of nuclear factor-Y transcription factor.
evidence.snippet: Mutant TATA box-binding protein binds more tightly to the transcription factor nuclear factor-Y, inhibits its association with the chaperone protein promoter, as well as the promoter activity and reduces the expression of the chaperones Hsp70, Hsp25 and HspA5, and their response to stress.

evidence.reference_title: Role of the CCAAT-binding protein NFY in SCA17 pathogenesis.
evidence.snippet: In SCA17 cells, HSPA5 promoter activity was activated as a compensatory response before aggregate formation. NFYA dysfunction was indicated in SCA17 cells as HSPA5 promoter activity reduced along with TBP aggregate formation.

nodes.description: Mutant TBP shows decreased association with the unfolded protein response transcription factor XBP1s, reducing transcription of the Purkinje-enriched neurotrophic factor MANF. Loss of this cell-type-specific neuroprotective signal contributes directly to the selective vulnerability of cerebellar Purkinje cells; restoring chaperone (Hsc70) activity or overexpressing MANF rescues Purkinje cell degeneration via PKC-dependent signaling.

biological_processes: regulation of transcription by RNA polymerase II

evidence.reference_title: Age-dependent decrease in chaperone activity impairs MANF expression, leading to Purkinje cell degeneration in inducible SCA17 mice.
evidence.snippet: Mutant TBP shows decreased association with XBP1s, resulting in the reduced transcription of mesencephalic astrocyte-derived neurotrophic factor (MANF), which is enriched in Purkinje cells.

evidence.reference_title: Age-dependent decrease in chaperone activity impairs MANF expression, leading to Purkinje cell degeneration in inducible SCA17 mice.
evidence.snippet: We find that more mutant TBP accumulates in older mouse and that this accumulation correlates with age-related decreases in Hsc70 and chaperone activity. Consistently, older SCA17 mice experienced earlier neurological symptom onset and more severe Purkinje cell degeneration.
